HCM.summary: HCM is the most common inherited cardiac condition. Two main genetic strategies are in development: gene replacement for MYBPC3 (which causes disease through protein deficiency) and CRISPR-based editing for MYH7 (which causes disease through dominant-negative mutant proteins).

approaches.description: TN-201 delivers a functional copy of the MYBPC3 gene via AAV9 vector to restore myosin-binding protein C levels in heart muscle cells.
approaches.status: Active — first-in-human trial; initial data shows good tolerability and increased protein expression. Clinical hold lifted Dec 2025.
approaches.key_finding: AAV9-mediated gene replacement can restore MyBP-C protein in humans. The critical question is whether sufficient protein levels translate to clinical improvement.

approaches.description: Adenine base editors (ABEs) precisely correct pathogenic MYH7 point mutations (e.g. p.Arg403Gln) by converting a single DNA nucleotide without creating double-strand breaks.
approaches.status: Animal models — successful correction in pre-symptomatic mice prevents cardiac hypertrophy and fibrosis.
approaches.key_finding: Single-dose base editing can prevent HCM phenotypes in animal models when delivered early. Human translation faces delivery and off-target challenges.

approaches.description: CRISPR-Cas9 or RNAi selectively disrupts the mutant MYH7 allele while leaving the healthy copy intact, stopping production of toxic dominant-negative protein.
approaches.status: In vitro and early animal studies — proof-of-concept demonstrated for allele-specific knockdown.
approaches.key_finding: Silencing the mutant allele is promising for dominant-negative MYH7 mutations, but each mutation may require a custom guide RNA.

LQTS.summary: LQTS gene therapy is less clinically advanced than HCM but has seen breakthrough preclinical results. The leading approach is suppression-replacement (SupRep), which silences the faulty gene and replaces it with a corrected copy in a single delivery.

approaches.description: A single AAV vector delivers both a shRNA to suppress endogenous KCNQ1 (both healthy and mutant copies) and a replacement KCNQ1 gene engineered to be immune to suppression.
approaches.status: Proven in transgenic rabbit models — normalised QT intervals and reduced arrhythmia risk. Moving toward IND-enabling studies.
approaches.key_finding: SupRep can normalise QT intervals in a large-animal model. The approach is gene-specific rather than mutation-specific, making it applicable to most LQT1 patients.

approaches.description: Same SupRep strategy adapted for KCNH2. The shRNA targets KCNH2 transcripts while a codon-optimised replacement gene restores functional hERG potassium channels.
approaches.status: Proof-of-concept in animal models. Behind LQT1 SupRep in development timeline.
approaches.key_finding: KCNH2 SupRep faces additional challenges due to the larger gene size and more complex splicing, but early results are promising.

approaches.description: For LQT3 (gain-of-function SCN5A mutations), precision drug therapy with mexiletine blocks the pathological late sodium current rather than editing the gene directly.
approaches.status: Clinical standard — mexiletine effectively shortens QT interval in LQT3 patients. Research into next-generation sodium channel blockers continues.
approaches.key_finding: LQT3 is currently managed pharmacologically rather than with gene therapy. Gene editing for SCN5A remains early-stage laboratory research.

FH.summary: FH has the most clinically advanced gene-targeted therapies of the three conditions. PCSK9-targeting approaches (both siRNA and base editing) are already in human trials, with inclisiran FDA-approved and VERVE-102 in Phase 1b.

approaches.description: Inclisiran is a small interfering RNA (siRNA) that inhibits PCSK9 production in the liver, increasing LDL receptor availability and lowering LDL cholesterol.
approaches.status: Approved for primary hypercholesterolaemia including HeFH. Administered as a twice-yearly injection. Reduces LDL-C by ~50%.
approaches.key_finding: Inclisiran is the first approved siRNA therapy for FH. Twice-yearly dosing dramatically improves adherence over daily statins.

approaches.description: VERVE-102 uses adenine base editor delivered via lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) to permanently inactivate the PCSK9 gene in liver cells with a single infusion.
approaches.status: Active — single infusion reduced PCSK9 and LDL-C by up to 53% in the 0.6 mg/kg cohort. Well-tolerated with no serious adverse events reported.
approaches.key_finding: First-in-human base editing shows that a one-time treatment can produce sustained LDL-C reduction. Long-term durability and safety monitoring continues.

approaches.description: AAV-mediated delivery of a functional LDLR gene to liver cells to restore LDL receptor expression in patients with homozygous FH (HoFH) who have little to no receptor function.
approaches.status: Early clinical trials — immune responses and transient expression in dividing liver cells remain significant hurdles. Being explored primarily for HoFH where no other options exist.
approaches.key_finding: Gene replacement for LDLR is most needed for HoFH but faces major delivery and durability challenges. Base editing of PCSK9 is currently more promising.
